title: Entanglement (1+2) QED in a double layer of Dirac Materials
authors: Facundo Arreyes, Federico Escudero, Arián Gorza, Sebastián Ardenghi
abstract: We investigate the momentum-space entanglement between two Dirac quasiparticles in a double-layer honeycomb lattice coupled via a planar electromagnetic cavity. We model the low-energy excitations as massive Dirac fermions in $(1+2)$ dimensions and derive the Bethe-Salpeter equation using the ladder approximation. We use a Born-level approximation around a free two-body quasiparticle state, where the interaction is mediated by the cavity photon propagator. From the reduced sublattice density matrix, we compute a momentum-resolved von Neumann entropy. Within the perturbatively controlled regime, the entropy remains small, while phenomenological self-energy dressing drives a crossover to strong enhancement of the entanglement entropy. Stationary entanglement is obtained only when the quasiparticle coherence time exceeds the photon propagation time between the layers. The maximum-entropy regime appears to be a viable method for achieving Bell-like states. These results demonstrate how self-energy renormalization, virtual particle exchange, and spinor geometry combine to reshape the entanglement landscape of Dirac materials.
summary: This paper explores how a microcavity can mediate entanglement between electrons in two layers of Dirac materials. By using a (1+2)D QED model, the authors show that while entanglement is low in simple perturbative regimes, accounting for self-energy effects leads to a significant boost in entanglement. They identify specific kinematic conditions and coherence requirements necessary to achieve Bell-like states. This provides a roadmap for using cavity-mediated interactions to engineer quantum information resources in 2D materials.
main_problem: Investigating the generation and control of momentum-space pseudospin entanglement between two Dirac quasiparticles in a double-layer honeycomb lattice coupled via a microcavity.
main_result: The study identifies a regime where self-energy renormalization drives a crossover to strong entanglement enhancement, potentially enabling the generation of Bell-like states, provided the quasiparticle coherence time exceeds the photon propagation time.
method: The authors use a (1+2)D QED framework, applying the Bethe-Salpeter equation within the ladder approximation and a Born-level treatment to calculate the first-order correction to the two-body wave function.
model_or_system: A double-layer of honeycomb lattice materials (like silicene or germanene) embedded in a planar electromagnetic microcavity, where low-energy excitations are modeled as massive Dirac fermions interacting via virtual photon exchange.
key_observables: Momentum-resolved von Neumann entanglement entropy (pseudospin/sublattice entanglement).
important_parameters: Fermi velocity, spin-orbit coupling (effective mass), interlayer distance, cavity mode structure, and the ratio of quasiparticle coherence time to photon propagation time.
main_assumptions: The study assumes a single valley, neglects interval valley scattering, uses a phenomenological approach for self-energy dressing, and employs the ladder approximation for photon exchange.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the theoretical setup and interaction channels; Figure 2 demonstrates convergence tests for mode cutoffs; Figure 3 illustrates entanglement entropy as a function of interlayer distance showing enhancement due to self-energy.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the Dirac field and cavity photon propagator, moves to the Bethe-Salpeter framework for the two-body state, calculates the reduced density matrix, and concludes with a discussion on the regimes of entanglement enhancement and suppression.
why_interesting: It bridges cavity QED and condensed matter by showing how vacuum-mediated interactions and self-energy effects can be engineered to create high-quality entanglement resources in solid-state systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic representation of the theoretical setup used in this work. Top: gapped Dirac-cone structure as- sociated with the honeycomb layers. Bottom: representa- tive cavity-mediated interlayer interaction channels used in the calculation. The diagrams summarize the four sectors entering the numerical construction of the two-body state— electron–electron (ee), electron–hole (eh), hole–electron (he), and hole–hole (hh)—all connected by virtual-photon exchange between the layers.

Figure 3. Entanglement entropy S1 as a function of the inter-layer distance d1 along the symmetric configuration d1+ d2 = L for fixed (Σ1, Σ2) and fixed kinematic configuration (p1, p2, ϕ1, ϕ2). Here we use Re Σ1 = Re Σ2 = 4.2 × 10−3 eV, chosen from Fig. 4 as a representative value near the entropy maximum while preserving the validity of the Born approxi- mation. All panels also use λso = 3.9 meV, corresponding to m ≃2.13 eV in the numerical convention.

Figure 2. Top: Entanglement entropy as a function of the inter-layer distances d1 and d2 for different values of the mode cutoff Nmax. Bottom: one-dimensional cuts along the symmetric configuration d1 + d2 = L. All panels use λso = 3.9 meV, corresponding to m ≃2.13 eV in the numeri- cal convention.

Figure 4. Entanglement entropy S1 as a function of the real parts of the self-energy parameters Σ1 and Σ2 (logarithmic axes, in eV), for fixed quasiparticle momenta p1 = 0.13 eV, p2 = 0.12 eV, collinear angles ϕ1 = ϕ2 = 0, and emitter positions d1 = 0.9, d2 = 1.1 eV−1 inside the cavity. The region to the left of it lies outside the perturbative regime. Results in that region are shown for completeness but should not be interpreted as physical predictions of the Born-level scheme. The dashed white line indicates Σ1 = Σ2. Parameters: λso = 3.9 meV (m ≃2.13 eV in natural units), Im(Σ) = 10−6 eV.

Figure 5. Entanglement entropy as a function of coherence time for Re Σ fixed. Top: a time window close to the light- flight time from one layer to the other. Bottom: values where the entanglement entropy stabilizes. Here we fix Re Σ1 = Re Σ2 = 4.2 ×10−3 eV, chosen from Fig. 4 as a representative value near the entropy maximum while preserving the validity of the Born approximation. All panels also use λso = 3.9 meV.
Figure 6. Entanglement entropy S1 as a function of the quasi- particle momenta p1 and p2 (in eV), for fixed collinear angles ϕ1 = ϕ2 = 0 and emitter positions d1 = 0.9, d2 = 1.1 eV−1
